fuzzy_prd: hey team, we need to build out that logging engine thing we talked about last sprint. basically the idea is devs should be able to fire off a log entry with some context attached and have it show up in one or more places without them having to do a bunch of wiring themselves. there's a bunch of stuff we need to handle — like, sometimes the message bytes are garbage and we don't want the whole thing to blow up, so we need to quietly fix that before it hits storage. also we talked about the thread thing, like how last time on the jobs service one noisy thread was drowning everything out — we need to be able to turn down the noise on just that thread without touching the rest. we also want the same entry to go to multiple destinations at once, kind of like how we set up the audit trail fan-out in the payments module. oh and there's a mode where you want the entry to go downstream but NOT get written to the main store — that needs to still fan out properly. entries should be able to carry extra context (stack info, environment stuff) without us manually passing it everywhere. the adapter layer should read commands from stdin as JSON and write results to stdout, same pattern as the notification dispatcher we built. deferred message producing should also be supported. check the existing test cases for the exact output format, i don't have time to spell it all out here.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: emit(level, message=nil, source=nil, metadata=nil, block=nil, skip_store=false, thread_id=nil)

trigger_keywords: how should invalid bytes be handled
what happens with malformed message encoding
garbled bytes in log message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a message contains byte sequences that are not valid in its declared encoding (we treat everything as UTF-8), each invalid byte run must be replaced with the Unicode replacement character U+FFFD before the entry is recorded. The repaired message is stored and the valid_encoding flag is set to true in the output — meaning the flag reflects whether the *stored* message is valid, not the original. The original invalidity is not surfaced beyond that flag. See feature2_encoding_scrub.json for the exact byte-level test: bytes [97, 32, 228, 32, 116, 101, 115, 116] — where 0xE4 (228) is not a valid standalone UTF-8 byte — should produce the output `message=a � test` with `valid_encoding=true`.

trigger_keywords: how does per-thread filtering work
thread-level severity threshold
can I set a log level for one thread only
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each thread may have its own severity threshold override. When a threshold is set (e.g. `thread_threshold: 2`), only entries whose numeric level is >= that threshold are recorded for that thread. Entries emitted from *other* threads that have no override are judged against the default, which accepts all levels (i.e. threshold of 0 / no threshold). Setting `thread_threshold: null` clears the override and restores the thread to accepting all levels. In the test adapter JSON protocol, the `thread_threshold` action applies to the 'main' thread, and individual log actions carry an optional `thread` field to specify which thread emits them. See feature1_threshold_override.json.

trigger_keywords: what does skip_store do
suppression flag behavior
entry goes downstream but not primary sink
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the skip_store flag is active, the primary sink must NOT record the entry (its total count stays unchanged). However, all attached downstream loggers must still receive the entry, AND the skip_store flag must be propagated to each downstream logger for the duration of that forwarded call. The downstream logger therefore sees `skip_store=true` alongside the entry. The primary sink's count output still appears in stdout — it just reflects zero recorded entries if nothing else was logged. See feature5_3_skip_store_propagation.json for the exact output: `chain_probe skip_store=true message=testing` followed by `primary total=0`.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for recorded entries
how should log lines be rendered
what does the primary sink output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each entry recorded by the primary sink is rendered as a single line: `primary level=<L> source=<tag> message=<text> backtrace=<bt> env=<json> valid_encoding=<flag>`. Absent/empty fields render as empty strings after the `=` (e.g. `source=`). The env field renders as a compact JSON object string when present, or empty. After all entries, a trailing line `primary total=<n>` reports the total count of recorded entries. For a chained recording sink the prefix is `chained` instead of `primary`. For the standard chain it is `chain_standard severity=<letter> message=<text>`. For the probe chain it is `chain_probe skip_store=<bool> message=<text>`. Each sink/downstream type has its own count.

trigger_keywords: what happens when message is null or missing
empty message behavior
no message provided to log call
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are two distinct cases. (1) If a deferred block/producer is provided instead of a message, the engine calls the producer to obtain the message text when the entry passes the severity gate — it is NOT called for dropped entries. (2) If neither a message nor a block is provided, the entry is still recorded but with an empty message. Crucially, when the message is empty (nil/absent, no block), the `valid_encoding` field must also render as empty (i.e. `valid_encoding=`) — there is no text to validate. When message is explicitly `null` in the JSON input but a source is present, the source is used as the message text for the standard chain formatter. See feature4_1_block_and_default_message.json and feature5_1_chain_standard.json.

trigger_keywords: how does fan-out to multiple sinks work
chaining downstream loggers
attaching additional loggers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The engine supports attaching one or more downstream loggers via a `chain` mechanism. When an entry is accepted (passes the severity gate), it is forwarded to each downstream logger BEFORE being recorded by the primary sink. There are three downstream logger types in the adapter: `recording` (same-kind sink, full payload, uses `chained` prefix), `standard` (foreign logger, single-letter severity indicator derived from level, uses `chain_standard` prefix), and `probe` (observes skip_store flag, uses `chain_probe` prefix). A failure in one downstream logger must not prevent delivery to others or to the primary sink. The severity letter mapping is: level 0→D, 1→I, 2→W, 3→E, 4→F (or similar — W for level 2 is confirmed by feature5_1). Downstream output appears before primary output in stdout.

trigger_keywords: where should JSON parsing live
should the core engine know about stdin or JSON
separation between adapter and engine
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core engine must have zero knowledge of JSON, stdin, or stdout. It exposes an in-process API only. A separate execution adapter layer is solely responsible for reading JSON commands from stdin, calling the core engine API, and writing rendered output to stdout. The JSON action types are: `log` (emit an entry), `thread_threshold` (set/clear per-thread threshold for 'main' thread), `chain` (attach a downstream logger), and `skip_store` (toggle suppression). Malformed adapter input must produce a normalized error line rather than a raw exception stack trace. This is a hard architectural constraint — see the NFR section of the PRD ('The core engine MUST NOT know about stdin/stdout or JSON').

trigger_keywords: how is metadata passed through
backtrace and env fields
structured context on a log entry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An entry may carry a `metadata` object containing a `backtrace` string and an `env` map of arbitrary key-value pairs. Both are preserved verbatim all the way to the sink — the engine must not interpret, reshape, or filter them. In the output, `backtrace=<text>` and `env=<json-object>` appear as labelled segments. The env map is serialized as a compact JSON object (no extra spaces). If metadata is absent, both fields render as empty (`backtrace=` and `env=`). See feature3_metadata_passthrough.json.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: kind of like how we set up the audit trail fan-out in the payments module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The downstream fan-out mechanism described in Feature 5: attaching downstream loggers via a `chain` action so each accepted entry is broadcast to all of them before reaching the primary sink. See feature5_1_chain_standard.json, feature5_2_chain_recording.json, feature5_3_skip_store_propagation.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the adapter layer should read commands from stdin as JSON and write results to stdout, same pattern as the notification dispatcher we built
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter module that parses JSON action arrays from stdin and writes rendered log lines to stdout. This is architecturally separate from the core engine, which only exposes an in-process API. See NFR constraint #2 in start.md ('The core engine MUST NOT know about stdin/stdout or JSON').

context_pointers.vague_reference: like how last time on the jobs service one noisy thread was drowning everything out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The per-thread severity threshold override described in Feature 1. A `thread_threshold` action sets a minimum numeric level for the 'main' thread; entries from other threads are unaffected. See feature1_threshold_override.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the ticket — you'll need to look at the existing test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the PRD in start.md to work out the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the ground truth for output formatting.

feature1_threshold_override.json.description: A per-thread severity threshold filters log entries: an entry whose numeric level is below the active threshold for the thread that emits it is dropped, while an entry at or above the threshold is recorded. A threshold override applies only to the thread that set it; entries emitted on other threads are unaffected, and clearing the override on a thread restores its default acceptance of all levels. Each recorded entry is rendered with its level, source tag, message, attached metadata, and an encoding-validity flag, followed by a count of how many entries were recorded.

cases.expected_output:
primary level=0 source=prog message=test backtrace=backtrace env={"a":"x"} valid_encoding=true
primary level=0 source=prog message=test backtrace=backtrace env={"a":"x"} valid_encoding=true
primary total=2

feature2_encoding_scrub.json.description: When a message carries a byte sequence that is not valid text in the declared encoding, the logger repairs the message before recording it by replacing each invalid byte run with the standard replacement character, so the stored message is always valid text. The output shows the repaired message and an encoding-validity flag confirming the stored message is now valid.

cases.expected_output:
primary level=4 source=prog message=a � test backtrace= env= valid_encoding=true
primary total=1

feature3_metadata_passthrough.json.description: Structured metadata attached to a log entry — a captured backtrace and an arbitrary environment map — is carried through unchanged to the recorded entry alongside the level, source tag, and message. The output renders each metadata field as its own labelled segment so callers can confirm the backtrace text and the environment map survived intact.

cases.expected_output:
primary level=0 source=prog message=test backtrace=backtrace env={"a":"x"} valid_encoding=true
primary total=1

feature4_1_block_and_default_message.jso.description: When an entry is logged without an explicit message but with a deferred message producer, the message is obtained from that producer and recorded. When an entry is logged with neither an explicit message nor a producer, it is still recorded but with an empty message. The output lists each recorded entry in order and a count, demonstrating that both forms are accepted and that the deferred producer's value is captured.

cases.expected_output:
primary level=2 source= message=test backtrace= env= valid_encoding=true
primary level=2 source= message= backtrace= env= valid_encoding=
primary total=2

feature4_2_default_source.json.description: When an entry is logged with only a level and a message and no source tag is supplied, the recorded entry's source tag defaults to empty rather than being inferred from the message or any other field. The output shows the entry with an empty source segment, confirming the default.

cases.expected_output:
primary level=0 source= message=test backtrace= env= valid_encoding=true
primary total=1

feature5_1_chain_standard.json.description: When a foreign downstream logger is attached, each accepted entry is forwarded to it through the standard logging interface. The downstream logger formats the entry independently, exposing a single-letter severity indicator and the message text. The output shows the forwarded entry as observed at the downstream logger, plus the entry recorded by the primary sink, confirming the entry travelled through the real downstream formatting path rather than being short-circuited.

cases.expected_output:
chain_standard severity=W message=boom
primary level=2 source= message=boom backtrace= env= valid_encoding=true
primary total=1

feature5_2_chain_recording.json.description: When another sink of the same kind is attached downstream, each accepted entry is forwarded to it with the full payload — level, source tag, message, and all attached metadata (backtrace and environment map) — preserved identically to what the primary sink records. The output shows the forwarded entry as recorded by the downstream sink followed by the entry recorded by the primary sink, each with its own count.

cases.expected_output:
chained level=0 source=prog message=test backtrace=backtrace env={"a":"x"} valid_encoding=true
chained total=1
primary level=0 source=prog message=test backtrace=backtrace env={"a":"x"} valid_encoding=true
primary total=1

feature5_3_skip_store_propagation.json.description: A suppression flag prevents the primary sink from recording an entry, yet the entry is still forwarded to any attached downstream logger, and the suppression flag itself is propagated to that downstream logger for the duration of the forwarded call. The output shows the downstream logger observing the entry with the suppression flag active, while the primary sink records nothing.
